item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it comes from here and is licensed under NC "Non-commercial". Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aw, poor Mr. Mummy… :-(

item.title: Clinical tone
item.content:encoded: For the sake of clinical tone in this article "AD" should be changed to "CE".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea of mellified man always grossed me out in a very serious way, and this built on that. I also like how what was supposedly sorcery turned out to be just a problem with blood type-very clever twist. And reading the comments, the concept of this being a well-intentioned idea that went horribly wrong (paralleling the failures of real-life famine relief efforts) is superb. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the description is way too long with too much emphasis on the "shiz will fluff u up, ppl w/o AB+" aspects

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What I love about this the most is that, since its original creation and use, part of it that was once considered magic has been explained by modern science: blood type rejection. It didn't kill everyone but the royal family because the royal family was royal and the honey was cursed, but because of an accident of genetics and biology that makes sense in the modern world. Eating it is like getting a nutrient-enriched blood transfusion of AB+ blood - if that was what it was, it would have been considered magic at the time, but now makes perfect scientific sense. It's still anomalous, though, because how the heck does eating mummy honey correlate to a blood transfusion? (And also, dancing honey skeleton.) I love it! It's a reminder that all this anomalous stuff the Foundation contains may not be impossible, our scientific understanding of the universe just isn't sufficient to explain it yet; and when science does advance far enough that these things stop being anomalous, they'll get released back into the wild, as it were.

Being excessively hung up on the original idea behind the creation of the SCP Foundation, I could not love this thing more due to its backstory. I knew deciding to make Smapti my role model was a good idea…New favorite scip, upvoted with the force of a thousand suns. <3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Ethic Committee section probably doesn't refer to the mummy, but to keeping people with the wrong blood type from accidentally eating the honey.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why exactly should consumption of this honey by compatible individuals be pending on an Ethics Committee decision? It's not that the man has to suffer by being woken up in order to get it, after all. Still, good stuff! +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Basically, the sheikh and his family were spared because they're the descendants of the mollified man, and thus, through some unlikely accident of genetics, share his rare blood type.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought it was just a play on Royal Jelly because the sheik and his family are spared. Great article even though it would apparently kill me with my A+ blood. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Was mentioned before, but it still bugs me: That's not an exponential increase.

But I liked it anyway. Upvoted :-)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice work, and a good take on the infamous feeder fetish MCF food aid supplements that showed up a few months ago.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait…

Too much physics. I'm broke now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll buy the first one. A rush to help resulting in a botched job fits my mental image and personal experience with charitable groups.

I still say it was negligent, but it does seem more realistic and plausible when thought of that way.

Here's hoping the MCF learns from their mistakes before they prompt some of those scenarios in "Visions Of A Better World".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Given as it seems pretty explicit that this is a process that had been used previously on other people, that is my preferred explanation too.
This one is particularly bountiful, due to some quirk, but the same quirk renders it deadly dangerous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gravity is a constant acceleration, an increasing acceleration is a step above that, and is therefore liters per hour per hour per hour

I'm pretty sure that isn't what was meant however.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really creative and ever so slightly horrific. Good use of the prompt, and I'm liking the discussion of the new GoI above.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, it should be measured in liters per hour squared, more properly liters per hour per hour, just like meters per second per second when measuring gravitation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I deliberately didn't go into the MCF's backstory with the mummy because the Foundation knows so little about the MCF right now, but it was deliberate that it was found in Ethiopia in 1985. The famine that happened there was one of the biggest and most-publicized humanitarian crises of the 20th century, and led to all kinds of attempts at well-intentioned humanitarian aid that didn't work out the way they were supposed to (tons of food relief going to soldiers and warlords instead of people, entire villages being forcibly relocated into communal farms, milk and dairy products being sent to people who were lactose-intolerant, the song "Do They Know It's Christmastime?", etc.) It's possible that upon discovering this mummy that produces endless honey, the MCF threw caution to the wind and decided they needed to feed as many people as possible because they were out there dying right now.

It's also possible they did some perfunctory tests and it just so happened that the only people they had sample the honey happened to be AB+, and that they either didn't bother translating the Arabic, or assumed it was just standard boilerplate curse inscriptions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My first thought is that 1176 isn't the first one of these mummies that the Manna Charitable Foundation has encountered, or for that matter used. Sure, they tested the first few, but they just fed people and keep producing honey, but maybe more slowly, so when they found another one with a spigot already installed that pumped it out in ever increasing rates, they said "Well, it looks like the one's we've got already, so lets start shipping out the honey." Then, when they figured out what was going on, they bugged out, leaving only the "bad" mummy behind. That's just my initial response, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We have GoIs that want to set the scps free, worship them, destroy them, weaponize them, and use them as pretentious art. I think using scps to help the unfortuante is a niche waiting to be filled.

One thing bugs me, and it's very minor. Wouldn't the MCF have done some research or testing on their own before shipping out the honey? Even a very brief reading of the inscriptions or experimenting with the honey directly should have told them that it will kill most people. Either they were incredibly negligent or they didn't care about the casualties. Neither fits well with a charitably minded group with enough world-savvy to discover and acquire anomalous items.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I ain't eating no mummy, don't care how nutritionable it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Guilty. I liked the idea of a GoI that tries to use SCPs to help people in the third world, and was surprised to find it had only been used in that one article (not counting a deleted article that was apparently written by a fat fetishist to get his motor running.) When I was trying to figure out this article, it popped back into my head and I decided it was a good angle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
owned by the Manna Charitable Foundation.

Wait what. The same thing that's supposedly responsible for SCP-1135?!

…seems we may have a new GoI, folks.

Still. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good stuff. One small thing: "viscous" would be more tone-appropriate than "syrup-like consistency".

That aside, is blood rejection strictly considered an allergic reaction? It makes sense, but i've never heard it phrased that way, and you have me sincerely curious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty good. :) Nicely done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nicely done. I especially enjoyed the addenda, which added a nice level of punch to this. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found this SCP kind of "meh," but it was pretty well written, so no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh, this sure is different. I like different. Upvoted!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted for a very knowledgeable and convincing work. I only have one suggestion:

the rate at which production accelerated continued to increase by approximately one liter per hour

An increase in the rate of acceleration should properly be measured in litres per hour cubed, I think, but it would probably be better to reword this to have a constant rate of change in production rather than measuring a rate of change of the rate of change. Either way I'm pretty sure it's not technically exponential. (But it's too late at night to be thinking about calculus.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is great. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Liked it very much. I'm not familiar with Bright's challenge. Can someone point me to it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So noted. Good catch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A good SCP, but I would like to point out a few minor inaccuracies:

1. The name Abdel is not in fact a single name: Abd means slave or servant, and el means "of" in this context. Therefore, Abdel ibn Salah is not a correct Arabic name, since the prefix Abdel must only come before one of God's names (hence Abdallah- God's servant).

2. late classical Hellenic- you are most likely referring to the Hellenistic period here, rather than the late Classical one. In this later period the Greek culture was most influential in Egypt, which seems to where the coffin is from.

It is nice to see the picture in a good article though, good show.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: New SCP, based on a Bright's challenge to use this as the basis of an article.
